coredata.dc:title: A hybrid approach to optimize engine performance and emission control in internal combustion engines using graphene quantum dots-enhanced biodiesel-diesel blends
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Srinivasan, D.R.
Rajesh, P.
Joshi, Saurabh Pradeep
Kulkarni, Kaustubh Ganesh
coredata.dc:description: Reducing harmful emissions while improving engine performance is a critical challenge in internal combustion (IC) engines. The usage of fossil diesel exacerbates environmental and health concerns, necessitating alternative fuel solutions. This study addresses this issue by proposing a hybrid deep learning model, WLSA-FEINN, to optimize and forecast engine performance and emissions utilizing carbon-doped graphene quantum dots (GQD) in biodiesel-diesel blends (BDB). The innovation of this research lies in integrating a global search algorithm (WLSA) with a physics-informed neural network (FEINN), creating a unique hybrid framework that not only improves predictive accuracy but also enhances the interpretability of fuel-performance relationships. The WLSA algorithm is employed to optimize key engine parameters, while FEINN ensures precise forecasting of engine performance and emissions from the exhaust. The proposed technique was implemented in MATLAB and compared against existing models, including Artificial Neural Networks (ANN), Recurrent Enhanced Recurrent Neural Networks (RERNN), and Deep Neural Networks (DNN). Results demonstrate that the B10 + E6 + GQD45 fuel blend improves brake power by 13.84 % compared to conventional diesel (D100), while reducing CO, UHC, and NOx emissions by 21.90 %, 25.48 %, and 27.34 %, respectively. The WLSA-FEINN model exhibited the highest prediction accuracy, achieving a Mean Squared Error (MSE) of 0.0001 and a Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) of 0.0065 for Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC). These findings indicate that GQD-enhanced biodiesel blends significantly improve performance while reducing environmental impact.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bio-diesel
Diesel
Dynamometer
Emission
Fuel
Fuel flow meter

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Configuration of biodiesel-diesel fuel IC engine
Synthesis, stabilization, and characterization of GQD for diesel-biodiesel fuel blends
Proposed WLSA-FEINN based prediction of performance and exhaust emissions in IC engine using biodiesel
Optimization using weighted leader search algorithm (WLSA)
Prediction of performance and exhaust emission by Finite element Interpolated Neural Networks (FEINN)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Biodiesel-Diesel Fuel IC Engine Performance and Emission Analysis.
Flow chart of the production of WFOB and its blends with diesel and GQD.
Flow Chart of WLSA.
Analysis of GQD produced nanoparticles.
Analysis of (a) Ultraviolet Visible of GQD produced nanoparticles and (b) BP analysis at three engine speeds using various fuel blends.
Analysis of (a) ET vs different fuel for various speeds and (b) BSFC vs different fuel for various speeds.
Analysis of (a) CO results from three engine speeds and various fuel blends and (b) CO2 different fuel for different speeds.
Analysis of (a) UHC vs different fuel for various speeds and (b) NOx vs different fuel for various speeds.
Analysis of Curvature of MSE to drill subset and test subset.

In the testing phase, the following are analyzed. (a) the WLSA-FEINN model compared to the pragmatic data; (b) the WLSA-FEINN model compared to the practical data; (c) the relative error was amended; and (d) the error histogram.
Analysis of the MLP classical Recital using CO data (a) number of iteration vs Carbon dioxide, (b) Observed data vs Predicted, (c) Error range vs Frequency.
Analysis of Recital of the MLP classical through data of NO (a) number of iteration vs NO, (b) Observed data vs Predicted, (c) Error Range vs Frequency.
Analysis of (a) CO with Load, (b) BSFC with Load, (c) NOx with Load.
Effect of Ambient Temperature on Engine Performance Parameters (a) CO Emissions, (b) NOx Emissions, (c) BSFC.
Comparison of Convergence.
Comparison table of Existing Methods.

row.entry: ANN model for forecasting a CI engine's output and emissions using diesel, biodiesel, and Karanja oil
With alternative fuels, ANN reliably forecasts emissions and system performance in micro-trigeneration systems.
limited to a single alternative fuel (Karanja biodiesel); no other renewable energy sources were investigated
RERNN was used to predict spark ignition engine performance.
RERNN approach yields high prediction accuracy for engine performance and emissions
huge dataset was necessary for efficient learning, yet there was no validation from actual use
Predicting transient NOx, soot, and hydrocarbon emissions in diesel engines using task transfer learning
Task transfer learning increases the precision of emissions predictions in engine circumstances that were transient.
Focuses only on emissions, not considering performance parameters
ANN-based prediction of performance and emissions in CI engines utilizing biodiesel from waste cooking oil.
Developed ANN models for engine performance and emissions prediction, minimizing physical testing.
Limited to specific biodiesel blends; may require further validation with diverse fuel compositions.
ANN modeling for predicting emissions and efficiency in mini-diesel engines.
Identified key performance metrics and environmental impacts, showcasing ANN's predictive efficiency.
Focused on mini-diesel engines, which may limit generalization to larger engine types.
GPR modeling for predicting performance and emissions of castor oil ethyl ester biodiesel blends.
Showed GPR as an effective tool for small dataset modeling, optimizing engine performance parameters.
Data limitations restrict model accuracy; requires further validation with larger datasets.
Predictive modeling and engine characteristic optimization in dual-fuel mode using biogas and biodiesel.
Used neural networks with Box–Behnken Design for optimizing engine parameters, improving fuel efficiency.
Limited to dual-fuel mode; can’t directly apply to single-fuel engines.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fuels Selected for Diesel Engine Characterization.
Emissions values for different fuel types at full load.
Model Evaluation for Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC).
MSE Comparison for BSFC and CO Predictions across Existing methods.
Uncertainty Assessment of Experimental Measurements.

head.ce:title: A hybrid approach to optimize engine performance and emission control in internal combustion engines using graphene quantum dots-enhanced biodiesel-diesel blends

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, JNTUA, Anantapuramu, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mechanical Engineering, JNTUA, Anantapuramu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Dr. Vishwanath Karad MIT World Peace University, Pune, Maharashtra 411038, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Dr. Vishwanath Karad MIT World Peace University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Dr. Vishwanath Karad MIT World Peace University, Pune, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Reducing harmful emissions while improving engine performance is a critical challenge in internal combustion (IC) engines. The usage of fossil diesel exacerbates environmental and health concerns, necessitating alternative fuel solutions. This study addresses this issue by proposing a hybrid deep learning model, WLSA-FEINN, to optimize and forecast engine performance and emissions utilizing carbon-doped graphene quantum dots (GQD) in biodiesel-diesel blends (BDB). The innovation of this research lies in integrating a global search algorithm (WLSA) with a physics-informed neural network (FEINN), creating a unique hybrid framework that not only improves predictive accuracy but also enhances the interpretability of fuel-performance relationships. The WLSA algorithm is employed to optimize key engine parameters, while FEINN ensures precise forecasting of engine performance and emissions from the exhaust. The proposed technique was implemented in MATLAB and compared against existing models, including Artificial Neural Networks (ANN), Recurrent Enhanced Recurrent Neural Networks (RERNN), and Deep Neural Networks (DNN). Results demonstrate that the B10 + E6 + GQD45 fuel blend improves brake power by 13.84 % compared to conventional diesel (D100), while reducing CO, UHC, and NOx emissions by 21.90 %, 25.48 %, and 27.34 %, respectively. The WLSA-FEINN model exhibited the highest prediction accuracy, achieving a Mean Squared Error (MSE) of 0.0001 and a Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) of 0.0065 for Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC). These findings indicate that GQD-enhanced biodiesel blends significantly improve performance while reducing environmental impact.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: This study is exempt from the data sharing policy because it does not contain any newly created or inspected data.

ce:section.ce:para: Fossil fuels have long served as a significant energy source throughout a range of industries, such as agriculture and transportation, due to their high energy efficiency, availability, compatibility, reliability [1]. However, the global energy landscape faces critical challenges, such as dwindling oil reserves and environmental pollution stemming from fossil fuel combustion. As a result, the transition to renewable energy resources with lower pollution levels is necessary to preserve natural resources and lessen the impact on the environment. [2]. With benefits over fossil diesel, including a greater certain number, enhanced flash point, and lower exhaust pollutants, biodiesel has become a viable substitute and renewable resource [3]. Biodiesel is mostly made from plant oils, both edible and inedible, by a process called transesterification. Triglycerides and free fatty acids (FFAs) are combined with a basic monohydric alcohol and a catalyst to create biodiesel [4]. For usage in diesel engines, this renewable fuel can be combined with traditional diesel. International organizations have acknowledged the advantages of biodiesel for the environment, especially its ability to reduce greenhouse gas emissions [5]. However, there are still issues, such as increased emissions of nitrogen oxide (NOx). The performance qualities of biodiesel have been proposed to be improved by the use of several additives, especially nanoparticles, in order to lessen these disadvantages. Nanoparticles are well-known for their special qualities, which increase their usefulness in a variety of fields, including energy [6]. Heat transfer efficiency and thermal conductivity are greatly increased when they are added to fuels. Research indicates that specific nanoparticles, such as manganese, ferrocene, carbon nanoparticles, Sr@ZnO, CuO, and nanographene oxide, can improve the performance of biodiesel blends in internal combustion engines [7]. For example, manganese nanoparticles improve the freezing point, viscosity, and overall engine performance of waste chicken fat biodiesel mixtures. Additionally, studies on biodiesel derived from cottonseed oil, when combined with varying ratios of octanol and functionalized Multi-Walled Carbon Nanotubes (MWCNT), demonstrate that the integration of MWCNT nanoparticles improves diesel engine efficiency and performance [8]. When combined with biodiesel and diesel, alcohols in particular, ethanol derived from organic sources like sugar beets and vegetable grains further increase engine efficiency and lower exhaust pollutants[21,22]. Public transportation, ethanol is an environmentally favourable option because it burns cleanly, producing just carbon dioxide and wate[23,24]r. The exhaust emissions from these alternative fuels are still higher those from fossil diesel, even with the advantages of biodiesel and its blends [9]. Researchers are looking at affordable remedies for this problem, such as carbon-doped Quantum Dots (QDs). These semiconductor-based nanocrystals have special qualities that can be adjusted for a range of uses. They are appropriate for use as additives in BDBs, improving performance and lowering emissions due to their remarkable electrical and thermal conductivity [10].

Although the incorporation of carbon-doped Quantum Dots (QDs) into mixtures of biodiesel and diesel has potential benefits in terms of enhancing engine efficiency and cutting emissions, a number of obstacles need to be overcome before this technology is widely used. There are still many obstacles to overcome in the production and scalability of carbon-doped QDs. It can be difficult to synthesize these nanoparticles economically while maintaining a high standard of quality, which might restrict their applicability in the marketplace. In order to avoid agglomeration, which could have a detrimental effect on performance, it is also necessary to thoroughly investigate the long-term stability and dispersibility of QDs in biodiesel-diesel mixes. Another problem is getting biodiesel-diesel mixes to work with current engine technology. The distinct characteristics of these mixes necessitate alterations to fuel systems or engine parts, which could result in higher expenses and possible operating problems. The environmental benefits from biodiesel, however, increased nitrogen oxide (NOx) emissions are still a cause for worry. To mitigate this problem without sacrificing efficacy, meticulous blend design and tuning are necessary. Ultimately, it is necessary to navigate regulatory obstacles and the market's adoption of biodiesel-diesel blends containing QDs because industries frequently hesitate to embrace new technologies that might not have set standards or demonstrated performance records.
a.
Literature Review
Numerous studies are presented on the employ of BDBs to predict the performance and exhaust emissions of IC engines. Here, a few of them are reviewed.

Khatri et al., [11] have introduced an ANN method for forecasting the emission parameters and performance of a micro-trigeneration system powered by CI engines and fed with diesel, Karanja biodiesel and Karanja oil. Experimental data from a test rig powered by diesel, Karanja Biodiesel (KB-20, KB-50), Karanja Oil, and a micro-tri-generation system were collected to measure emissions and performance. Performance from the tri-generation system was on par with diesel. An ANN-based method was created to forecast micro-tri-generation emissions and performance for various alternative fuel sources using data from single-generation systems.

Kumar et al., [12] have demonstrated the RERNN technique for forecasting the spark ignition engines' emissions and performance. Additionally, the RERNN model was trained via an improved conjugate approach that leverages a generalized Armijo search strategy to accelerate convergence. The suggested method was used to analyse emissions from engine-outs, combustion phasing, mean effective pressure, exhaust temperature, burn length, maximum pressure rise rate, and ignition lag.

Shin et al., [13] presented a task transfer learning method to forecast a diesel engine's emissions of soot, total hydrocarbons, and transient nitrogen oxides. The concept of task transfer learning allows one to transfer a model that was pertinent to the target task and forecast another event by training just the last 2 layers of a pre-trained model with hyper parameters from the pre-trained model. The idea eliminates the need to train and optimize individual models, which can reduce computing expenses and time.

Patnaik et al., [14] presented using blends of biodiesel produced from leftover cooking oil, an ANN technique to predict the emissions and efficiency of compression ignition engines. The study aimed to reduce resource-intensive testing by using property-based predictions. It determined which ANN topologies were best for accurately predicting exhaust gas temperature, BSFC, BSEC, and brake thermal efficiency.

Odufuwa et al., [15] have presented Mini-diesel engine efficiency and emissions can be predicted using ANN modeling. Emissions of pollutants including carbon monoxide (CO), carbon dioxide (CO2), nitrogen oxides (NOx), and particulate matter (PM) were evaluated, along with critical performance metrics like specific fuel consumption (SFC) and brake thermal efficiency (BTE).

Ariyarit et al., [16] presented the forecasting of emissions and engine performance for blends of castor oil and ethyl ester biodiesel. A single-cylinder diesel engine operating at 1500 rpm with compression ratios of 16, 17, and 18 were used to investigate the effects of an 8 % castor oil ethyl ester (COEE8) biodiesel-diesel blend on engine performance, emissions, and combustion. The best engine parameters were found using NSGA-II optimization and GPR modeling, which also addressed data constraints.

Ahmad and Singh, [17] presented a dual-fuel mode powered by biogas and biodiesel that optimizes engine characteristics through predictive modeling. A three-level, four-factor L29 orthogonal array was constructed using the Box–Behnken design (BBD). Using a B20 blend at 75 % load, experiments were conducted to examine various compression ratios, injection pressures, timings, and biogas flow rates. To forecast important engine performance and emission traits, an ANN model was created. The comparison of each previous study is given in the Table 1 .

Traditional predictive modeling methods for engine performance and emissions often lack optimization techniques, leading to suboptimal predictions for key factors, including fuel consumption, brake power, and emissions. Existing approaches, such as standalone neural networks and Gaussian Process Regression, do not incorporate a global search optimization mechanism, limiting their ability to dynamically fine-tune engine parameters. These methods often suffer from high computational costs and overfitting issues, which reduce their generalizability. While biodiesel-diesel blends have been extensively analyzed, the potential of nanomaterials like carbon-doped GQD as combustion enhancers remains largely unexplored, particularly in their role in optimizing combustion efficiency and reducing emissions. Most studies focus on steady-state engine conditions, neglecting variations such as variable load, transient operations, and speed fluctuations, which are critical for real-world applications. Addressing these gaps requires integrating advanced machine learning techniques with robust optimization frameworks to enhance prediction accuracy and practical applicability.

To address existing limitations, this study proposes a novel hybrid deep learning framework, WLSA-FEINN, which integrates the Weighted Leader Search Algorithm with Finite Element Interpolated Neural Networks to optimize internal combustion engine performance using blends of biodiesel and diesel enhanced with carbon-doped graphene quantum dots. Unlike previous methods, this approach incorporates a global search-based optimization to dynamically fine-tune key engine parameters, improving fuel efficiency and reducing emissions across variable load and speed conditions. This study pioneers the use of carbon-doped GQD in biodiesel-diesel blends, demonstrating its enhanced combustion properties, which were previously unexplored. These advancements contribute to lower fuel consumption, reduced environmental impact, and extended engine lifespan, making the framework highly applicable for sustainable transportation, industrial engines, and future clean-energy solutions.

The following is a summary of the work's contribution:
•
The paper introduces an innovative hybrid method that integrates the Weighted Leader Search Algorithm with Finite Element Interpolated Neural Networks to effectively model interactions between fuel composition and engine parameters, enhancing performance and reducing harmful emissions in IC engines.
•
The approach utilizes the WLSA to fine-tune the working parameters of the engine, while FEINN accurately forecasts the performance of the engine and emissions related to the blends of biodiesel and diesel.
•
The incorporation of carbon-doped GQD into BDBs improves combustion efficiency and significantly decreases harmful emissions, addressing critical environmental concerns.
•
The research explores the potential of using GQD-enhanced biodiesel-diesel blends as a sustainable fuel solution, offering an environmentally friendly alternative to traditional fossil fuels.
•
The study demonstrates that the GQD-enhanced biodiesel blends result in significant reductions in harmful exhaust emissions, including CO, UHC, and NOx, contributing to cleaner engine operation.
•
The WLSA-FEINN technique's performance is closely contrasted with various existing approaches, allowing for a robust assessment of its effectiveness and potential advantages.

The rest of the document is arranged as follows: configuration of biodiesel-diesel fuel IC engine is covered in Part 2. The proposed methods are clarified in Part 3. The result and discussion are explained in Part 4. Part 5 presents the conclusion at last.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Configuration of biodiesel-diesel fuel IC engine
ce:section.ce:para: The performance of a biodiesel-diesel fuel blend was assessed in this study using an internal combustion (IC) engine setup. The engine was first run for roughly 13 min on pure diesel fuel to get rid of any undesirable contaminants to get ready for the performance evaluation. The engine was then tested using different fuel mixtures. At various engine speeds under full load conditions, performance metrics including exhaust emissions (carbon monoxide (CO), nitrogen oxides (NOx), carbon dioxide (CO2 and unburned hydrocarbons (UHC)), BSFC, braking power (BP), engine torque (ET) were assessed.

Biodiesel-Diesel Fuel IC Engine Performance and Emission Analysis is displayed in Fig. 1 . The process starts with the test engine receiving the fuel mixture from the gasoline tank. While exhaust gases are routed to an exhaust gas analysis system for immediate time emissions monitoring, engine performance measurements are recorded by a dynamometer connected to a load control module. In order to optimise engine factors for superior performance and lower emissions, a computer uses the WLSA-FEINN hybrid technique to analyse the exhaust system's emission data and the dynamometer's performance data.

A dynamometer can be employed to measure an engines' output of torque, force, or power and other rotating machinery. In order to calculate power, it simultaneously measures torque and rotational speed (RPM). Different dynamometer types, such as driving and absorption types, are used for different kinds of tests. Applications include industrial machinery evaluation and automotive testing, enabling engineers to maximize efficiency and guarantee adherence to legal requirements. Dynamometers are also essential for research and development in a variety of industries, thanks to the useful data they provide for enhancing mechanical systems.

An indispensable tool for determining the flow rate of liquid fuels, including gasoline, diesel, and biodiesel, in a variety of applications is a fuel flow meter. These meters give businesses including manufacturing, transportation, and agricultural access to real time fuel consumption data, which facilitates effective monitoring and optimization of fuel use. Typical varieties include turbine, positive displacement, and ultrasonic flow meters; each has unique benefits depending on application requirements and accuracy. These meters are essential tools for fleet management and industrial operations because they precisely trace fuel flow, which helps cut waste and increase operational efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis, stabilization, and characterization of GQD for diesel-biodiesel fuel blends
ce:section.ce:para: This “bottom-up” method, which uses the thermal breakdown of citric acid gas, was used to produce graphene quantum dots (GQD). GQDs with desired qualities for a range of applications are efficiently produced using this procedure. To preserve their integrity and avoid agglomeration, the GQDs were stabilized in ethanol after synthesis. Among the techniques employed to investigate the characteristics of the synthesized GQDs were Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis), Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR), and High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HR-TEM). These analytical techniques shed light on the GQDs' optical and structural characteristics, which are essential for their potential uses in bio imaging, sensing, and catalysis. The fuels selected for the diesel engine characterization are displayed in Table 2 .

Biodiesel was first made and mixed with diesel at a 10 % ratio. An ultrasonic homogenizer was then used to stabilize the synthesized graphene quantum dots (GQD) in various ethanol percentages. Each composition was ultrasonically agitated for 20 min. After stabilizing, these GQD-ethanol blends were added to the diesel–biodiesel fuel blend. The flow chart of the production of WFOB and its blends with diesel and GQD is shown in Fig. 2 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposed WLSA-FEINN based prediction of performance and exhaust emissions in IC engine using biodiesel
ce:section.ce:para: This study presents a hybrid approach that uses BDBs enhanced with carbon-doped quantum dots to forecast the performance and internal combustion engine (IC) exhaust emissions. The proposed approach combines the Weighted Leader Search Algorithm (WLSA) with Finite Element Interpolated Neural Networks (FEINN) to work in parallel. The primary objective of the WLSA-FEINN technique is to accurately model interactions between fuel composition and engine parameters, optimizing performance and reducing internal combustion engines' harmful emissions. WLSA is utilized to fine-tune the working parameters, while FEINN is responsible for accurately predicting the engines emissions and performance associated with the biodiesel-diesel blends.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization using weighted leader search algorithm (WLSA)
ce:section.ce:para: In this section, WLSA [18] is used to optimize the working parameters of the IC engine. It efficiently strikes a balance between exploitation and exploration, allowing for effective search in intricate parameter spaces. Influential parameters are prioritized using a weighted method by WLSA, which improves optimization accuracy. WLSA enhances optimization efficiency by dynamically adjusting search weights, allowing it to identify the most influential parameters and refine them iteratively. This ensures that the model achieves a globally optimal solution rather than settling at a local minimum, thereby improving overall prediction accuracy.
Step 1: Initialization
Set up the engine regulator's factors, including speed and fuel combinations.
Step 2: Random Generation

Through WLSA, the input fitness function acquired randomness after initialization.

Step 5: Exploitation Phase.

Exploitation is essential for maximizing the prediction models' parameters. The algorithm predicts quality metrics like Brix and Pol more accurately by focusing on the most important spectral features and associated weights. The WLS method employs two exploitation strategies: solutions that are generated by the objective function's descent direction and that are chosen at random, both of which compute

Step 6: Termination

With the help of WLSA, the IC engine's operating parameters were optimised. Step 3 will be repeated iteratively until the halting criteria

ce:section.ce:section-title: Prediction of performance and exhaust emission by Finite element Interpolated Neural Networks (FEINN)
ce:section.ce:para:
In this sector prediction using FEINN [19] is discussed. By simulating the connections between input engine parameters and output performance metrics, the performance and the IC engine's exhaust emissions are predicted by employing FEINN. Finite element analysis and neural networks are combined to create FEINN, an efficient way to describe intricate correlations between engine behaviour and input parameters. By using spatial interpolation techniques, which improve the neural network's capacity to learn from data produced by finite element simulations, this method enables excellent prediction accuracy. Appropriate boundary conditions are applied to account for fixed supports, loads, and constraints on the engine, which is given in equation (7).
(7)

)
)
L 1
( Ω )
where
B h
- 1
represents the inverse of the discrete Laplacian,
R h
represents the isomorphic variable. Finally, FEINN forecasted the IC engine's performance and exhaust emissions, which increased engine torque and brake power while lowering nitrogen oxides, carbon monoxide, and unburned hydrocarbons.

This sector evaluates the effectiveness of the WLSA-FEINN approach according to the results of the simulation used to estimate exhaust emissions and IC engine performance [20]. The primary goal of the proposed method is to enhance internal combustion engine performance and forecast exhaust emissions. The implementation of the proposed strategy was conducted on the MATLAB platform, and its effectiveness was compared with alternative methods, including ANN, RERNN and DNN. To ensure the accuracy and reliability of the results, all simulations were repeated three times, and the average outcomes were analyzed for consistency and robustness.

Fig. 4 presents the transmittance spectra of nanoparticles synthesized via GQD. Graph (a) shows a sharp peak near 3000 cm−1 and several smaller peaks between 1500 cm−1 and 1000 cm−1, indicating distinct vibrational modes. In contrast, graph (b) displays smoother, more uniform peaks with less variation. The peak near 3000 cm−1 appears in both graphs but is less pronounced in (b). These differences suggest variations in sample characteristics, such as size distribution or surface functionalization, affecting optical properties and transmittance behavior. Fig. 5 presents (a) the ultraviolet–visible absorbance spectrum of GQD-produced nanoparticles and (b) brake power (BP) analysis for various fuel blends at different engine speeds. In graph (a), absorbance peaks just above 2 at ∼ 200 nm and gradually decreases toward 1000 nm, indicating strong UV absorption. Graph (b) compares BP at 1800, 2100, and 2400 rpm, showing slight variations across fuel blends, ranging from ∼ 4 to just under 6 kW. Overall, GQD nanoparticles exhibit excellent UV absorption, while fuel blends maintain consistent brake power efficiency across different engine speeds. Fig. 6 compares engine performance parameters across different speeds and fuel blends. Graph (a) shows engine torque (ET) increasing with speed, ranging from ∼ 20 Nm for D100 to ∼ 25 Nm for B10 + E8 + GQD45 at 2400 rpm. Graph (b) illustrates BSFC decreasing with speed, with values at 2400 rpm lower for B10 + E8 + GQD45 and exceeding 300 g/kWh for D100. Overall, fuel blends with GQD additives enhance efficiency, as higher engine speeds result in increased ET and reduced BSFC, highlighting their potential for improved engine performance and fuel economy. Fig. 7 compares CO and CO2 emissions for various fuel blends at different engine speeds. Graph (a) shows CO emissions peaking at 1800 rpm, especially for D100 and B10, while B10 + E8 + GQD45 records the lowest CO emissions. Graph (b) illustrates CO2 emissions slightly increasing with speed, with most fuels reaching peak levels at 2400 rpm, except B10 + E2 + GQD45, which remains stable. Overall, GQD additives help reduce CO emissions at lower speeds, while higher speeds lead to a marginal CO2 rise. Lower speeds generally result in increased CO emissions across all fuel blends. Fig. 8 illustrates UHC and NOx emissions for various fuel blends at different engine speeds. Graph (a) shows UHC emissions ranging from 60 to 160 ppm, with D100 peaking at 1800 rpm and B10 + E8 + GQD45 recording the lowest at 2400 rpm. Graph (b) depicts NOx emissions increasing with speed, reaching 80 ppm for D100 at 2400 rpm, while B10 + E8 + GQD45 records the lowest values. Overall, GQD additives aid emission control, reducing UHC while higher speeds tend to increase NOx emissions.

Fig. 9 presents the MSE trends for training, validation, and test subsets over 35 epochs. The y-axis uses a logarithmic scale (10−30 to 10−10). Training and validation lines remain stable, while the test subset spikes around epoch 30, indicating potential overfitting. The “Best” and “Goal” lines set benchmarks for performance. Despite stable training and validation, the test subset's anomaly suggests refinements are needed to enhance model reliability and prevent overfitting. Fig. 10 presents the performance analysis of the WLSA-FEINN model during testing. Graph (a) shows predictions across 50 iterations, ranging from −0.9 to 0.9. Graph (b) compares predicted values from −0.2 to 0.8 with observed data from 0.5 to 1. Graph (c) displays relative error amendments, with errors between −0.1 and 0.1. Graph (d) presents an error histogram cantered around zero, indicating minimal prediction errors. Collectively, these analyses demonstrate the model’s accuracy and reliability, showing that the WLSA-FEINN model effectively forecasts results with negligible deviations when compared to real-world data, confirming its strong predictive capabilities. Fig. 11 presents three key graphs from the CO data analysis using the MLP model. Graph (a) illustrates the correlation between iterations and CO levels, ranging from 1.3 to 1.9 mol per liter across 50 iterations. Graph (b) compares observed and predicted values, both within the same range, with error bars indicating prediction variability. Graph (c) depicts error distribution, showing most errors between −0.05 and 0.05, peaking at a frequency of 6. These findings highlight the model’s accuracy, demonstrating that discrepancies between observed and predicted data are minimal, confirming the MLP model’s efficiency and reliability in forecasting CO levels. Fig. 12 presents three key graphs from the MLP classical recital analysis using NO data. Graph (a) illustrates NO levels across 50 iterations, ranging from 66 to 78 ppm. Graph (b) compares observed and predicted values, both spanning 66 to 80 ppm, highlighting the model’s accuracy. Graph (c) displays error distribution, with most errors between −6 and + 6 ppm, peaking at a frequency of 2.5. The reliability and efficacy of the MLP method in forecasting NO levels with high accuracy and consistency are demonstrated by these results, which validate that there are few differences between observed and predicted values. Fig. 13 illustrates the impact of biodiesel blends enhanced with GQDs on engine performance and emissions under varying loads. CO emissions (a) increase with load for D100 but remain significantly lower in B10 and GQD-enhanced blends, staying below 0.06 % even at high load. BSFC (b) decreases across all fuels as load increases, with B10-GQD blends achieving the lowest values of 0.4 kg/kWh, indicating enhanced fuel efficiency. NOx emissions (c) rise with load but remain below 700 ppm for biodiesel blends with higher ethanol and GQD content. These findings highlight the potential of GQDs in enhancing engine efficiency and decreasing emissions. Fig. 14 illustrates the influence of ambient temperature on engine performance. Graph (a) shows that CO emissions increase with temperature, rising from ∼ 19 to 23.5 g/kWh at 1200 RPM. Graph (b) indicates NOx emissions also increase, from ∼ 7 to 9.5 g/kWh. In contrast, Graph (c) shows that BSFC decreases from ∼ 257 to 244 g/kWh. These trends suggest that while higher ambient temperatures lead to increased emissions, they also enhance fuel efficiency by reducing BSFC, highlighting a trade-off between emissions and fuel consumption. Fig. 15 compares the convergence of WLSA-FEINN, ANN, RERNN, and DNN models by plotting MSE over Epochs. Initially, all models show high MSE, which decreases as training progresses. WLSA-FEINN demonstrates the fastest drop in MSE, reaching a significantly lower value of approximately 0.17 compared to ANN at 0.29, RERNN at 0.40, and DNN at 0.52 after around 40 epochs, indicating superior convergence.

Table 3 presents CO and UHC emissions for various fuel types under full-load conditions. D100 emits 0.0150 g/kWh of CO and 22.30 g/kWh of UHC. Introducing biodiesel blends reduces UHC to 21.20 g/kWh but increases CO to 0.0280 g/kWh. Ethanol and GQD treatments influence emissions, with B10 + E2 + GQD45 emitting 0.0389 g/kWh CO and 16.69 g/kWh UHC, while B10 + E8 + GQD45 achieves the lowest UHC at 13.7 g/kWh but CO at 0.0415 g/kWh. Results highlight that biodiesel blends consistently lower UHC emissions, though CO emissions tend to rise, demonstrating the trade-off in optimizing environmental performance with alternative fuel combinations.

The effectiveness of several models in forecasting BSFC, expressed in kg/kWh, is assessed in Table 4 . An RMSE of 0.0065 and the lowest Mean Squared Error (MSE) of 0.0001 demonstrate the superior prediction accuracy of the proposed WLSA-FEINN model. The RERNN displays a substantially higher MSE of 0.0004 and RMSE of 0.0213, whereas the ANN displays an MSE of 0.0002 and an RMSE of 0.0092. With an RMSE of 0.0104 and an MSE of 0.0005, the DNN is in the middle. The WLSA-FEINN model performs better overall than the other approaches, indicating that it offers the most accurate BSFC predictions, which is essential for maximizing engine performance and fuel economy.

The Table 5 presents MSE values for two parameters, BSFC and CO predicted by the proposed WLSA-FEINN model. The MSE values indicate the prediction accuracy of the model, with lower values representing better accuracy. The WLSA-FEINN model achieves an MSE of 0.0001 for BSFC prediction and 0.012 for CO prediction, demonstrating its high accuracy in predicting both parameters. These outcomes highlight the effectiveness of the proposed technique in comparison to other methods, which typically exhibit higher MSE values.

The accuracy and reliability of experimental measurements are critical for ensuring the validity of the results. An uncertainty analysis was carried out for the primary performance and emission parameters in order to evaluate the accuracy of the measurements. The overall uncertainty (OU) was calculated using the root sum square (RSS) method, which accounts for the individual uncertainties of each measured parameter. The overall uncertainty is given in equation (12)
(12)
O U
%
= S Q R
UNC
o f
B P
2
+
UNC
o f
B T
2
+
UNC
o f
C O
2
+
UNC
o f
C
O 2
2
+
UNC
o f
U H C
2
+
UNC
o f
N
O x
2
The individual uncertainties for each parameter, as determined from the experimental setup and measurement instruments, are shortened in Table 6 . The total percentage uncertainty was computed to be ± 2.77 %, representing a high level of precision and reliability in the experimental results.

This study aims to explore innovative methods for improving the performance and sustainability of IC engines, particularly in the context of using alternative fuels. WLSA- A hybrid approach called FEINN predicts the performance and exhaust emissions of IC engines utilizing BDBs enhanced with GQD. The approach integrates WLSA for optimizing engine parameters with FEINN for forecasting engine performance and emissions. A 13.84 % increase in brake power is achieved with the B10 + E6 + GQD45 blend compared to D100, along with reductions in CO, UHC, and CO2 emissions by 21.90 %, 25.48 %, and 27.34 %, correspondingly. The WLSA-FEINN model demonstrated superior predictive accuracy, achieving an MSE of 0.0001 and RMSE of 0.0065 for BSFC, outperforming traditional methods such as ANN, RERNN, and DNN. The WLSA-FEINN model achieves an MSE of 0.0001 for BSFC prediction and 0.012 for CO prediction, demonstrating its high accuracy in predicting both parameters. It also achieved an MSE of 0.012 for CO emissions prediction, showcasing its high accuracy in both engine performance and emission forecasting outperforming existing methods. Additionally, a maximum engine torque of approximately 25 Nm at 2400 rpm for the B10 + E8 + GQD45 blend was recorded, with lower BSFC values at 2400 rpm compared to D100, indicating enhanced fuel efficiency. The findings emphasize the potential of GQD-improved biodiesel-diesel blends for sustainable fuel applications by demonstrating their efficacy in enhancing engine performance and lowering emissions within the WLSA-FEINN framework. Despite the potential of carbon-doped GQDs in biodiesel-diesel blends, challenges remain. High production costs limit scalability, while stability and dispersibility issues affect long-term performance. Compatibility with existing engines requires further study, as injector clogging and deposition risks remain uncertain. Regulatory concerns regarding emissions and environmental impact must also be addressed. Potential solutions include cost-effective synthesis methods, enhanced dispersion techniques, adaptive engine modifications, and comprehensive toxicity studies.

In conclusion, this manuscript presents an innovative hybrid strategy that incorporates the WLSA with FEINN to forecast the performance and exhaust emissions of an IC engine utilizing BDBs enhanced with carbon-doped quantum dots. Unlike conventional models that rely on static parameter tuning, the proposed approach leverages WLSA’s adaptive search strategy to dynamically optimize key engine parameters, ensuring improved prediction efficiency. The proposed hybrid method is implemented on the MATLAB platform. The proposed method demonstrates significant improvements in engine performance, with the maximum ET for the B10 + E8 + GQD45 blend reaching approximately 25 Nm at 2400 rpm. It achieves lower BSFC values at 2400 rpm compared to D100, indicating enhanced fuel efficiency. It attains a low MSE of 0.0001 for BSFC compared to traditional methods such as ANN, RERNN, and DNN. While carbon-doped GQDs show promise in biodiesel-diesel blends, several challenges remain. High production costs, stability and dispersibility issues, engine compatibility concerns, and regulatory challenges must be addressed to facilitate practical implementation. Additionally, external factors such as humidity and engine aging have not been extensively analyzed in this study. Future research will focus on scalable production methods for GQDs and interdisciplinary collaborations to enhance their practical adoption while ensuring regulatory compliance and long-term viability. The model will be validated across different engine types, regional fuel variations, and transient conditions to assess its robustness. A comprehensive economic feasibility study of GQDs in fuel applications will also be conducted to determine their commercial viability. The influence of humidity and engine aging on engine performance and emissions will be explored for a more comprehensive assessment.
No specific grant from a public, private, or nonprofit funding organization was given for this study.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
